food_name: Bánh Đa Cua (Vietnamese Red Rice Noodle Crab Soup)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bánh đa cua. The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic noodle soup food product class. Exact Vietnamese rice noodle soup terms are not present in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup dishes — rice noodle based
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for bánh đa cua. A0CKM (noodle soup dishes) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a rice-noodle-based soup dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese rice noodle soup dishes as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of bánh đa cua weighs approximately 400 g and constitutes a complete meal serving as typically sold at Vietnamese restaurants and street stalls.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia and Southeast Asia — rice noodle soup composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for bánh đa cua exists in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian food composition data for rice noodle soup dishes with mixed protein (crab, pork, shrimp) and vegetables. Input values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA rice noodle soup composites and retained where plausible. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where FAO/INFOODS SEA does not report disaggregated fatty acid data for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles, boiled (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bánh đa cua. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category), which is the primary carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 50.0) / 100 = 30.5, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 12.5 × 4 = 50.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content of 300 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per 400 g bowl) driven by fish sauce and pork broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, ask for broth served on the side, and limit portion size to half a bowl to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~61) from refined red rice noodles; moderate carbohydrate load of 50 g per standard 400 g bowl
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to half a bowl, request additional non-starchy vegetables such as water spinach to increase fibre and slow glucose absorption, and pair with protein-rich toppings (shrimp, crab) to moderate the postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg/100 g and cholesterol at 30 mg/100 g from crab, pork, and shrimp; saturated fat at 1.5 mg/100 g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose shrimp-only or crab-only versions to reduce saturated fat from pork, request lighter broth to lower sodium, and limit consumption to once weekly given the combined sodium and cholesterol load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) which may amplify inflammatory pathways; chilli is a nightshade that may trigger sensitivity in some RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute pork with shrimp-only or tofu, request omission of chilli, and increase vegetable toppings to improve antioxidant intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh đa cua is a traditional Vietnamese noodle soup originating from Hai Phong in northern Vietnam. The dish features bánh đa — thick, chewy red-brown rice noodles made from a blend of rice flour and gac fruit or red rice, which give them their distinctive reddish colour and slightly firmer texture compared to standard white rice noodles (bún). The broth is typically a rich, savoury pork and crab stock seasoned with fish sauce, shrimp paste, and aromatics. Toppings include freshwater crab meat (cua đồng), pork, shrimp, water spinach (rau muống), morning glory, and fresh herbs. The dish is assembled by placing cooked noodles in a bowl, ladling hot broth over them, and adding toppings. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g bowl provides approximately 450 kcal, 25 g protein, 15 g fat, and 50 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at approximately 1200 mg per bowl due to fish sauce and pork broth, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the rice noodle base is approximately 61 (medium), and the glycemic load per bowl is approximately 30.5, which is significant for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean or shellfish allergies. It is a culturally significant street food and restaurant dish widely available in Vietnamese communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: Is Vietnamese crab noodle soup high in sodium?
What are the calories in a bowl of bánh đa?
What is the difference between bánh đa and bún?
What allergens are in bánh đa cua?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for bánh đa cua exists in any approved primary source (FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore). Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA rice noodle soup composites and cross-checked against the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from boiled rice noodles per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: disaggregated fatty acid data for bánh đa cua are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this dish type in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. All core macros (protein_g 6.25, fat_g 3.75, carbohydrate_g 12.5) are non-zero and reflect the mixed protein-carbohydrate composition of this rice noodle soup dish with crab, pork, shrimp, and vegetables. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.03 g/100 g, a trace level consistent with minor contributions from pork fat in the broth. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, which likely reflects the restaurant context (GrabFood Singapore listing); the dish itself originates from Hai Phong, Vietnam, and this has been corrected in region_of_origin. The input source (FAO/INFOODS) was listed as supporting only menu_listing; nutrient values from the input (provenance: ai_generated) have been retained as estimated values cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA composites. Follow-up: a laboratory analysis or HPB-commissioned nutrient analysis of bánh đa cua as served in Singapore would substantially improve confidence.
